Security.title: API keys and credentials may be leaked in error logs and tracebacks
Security.description: The exception handler walks the entire exception object via `sanitize()` and dumps it with `json.dumps(..., indent=2)`. Many LiteLLM exception objects carry `api_key`, `api_base`, and full request payloads as attributes (`message`, `response`). These are written into the server log at ERROR level.
Security.impact: Credential leakage via logs is one of the most common real-world secrets exposures. A single DEBUG or ERROR dump of an upstream 401 will surface the configured API key.
Security.explanation: An explicit deny-list for credential-bearing attribute names prevents the most common forms of accidental key leakage.

Security.title: No authentication or authorization on proxy endpoints
Security.description: Both `/v1/messages` and `/v1/messages/count_tokens` accept arbitrary unauthenticated requests. The server binds to `0.0.0.0:8082`, so any reachable client can call the proxy and consume the configured provider quota.
Security.impact: A proxy that holds paid API credentials but exposes its own endpoints without auth is an open relay for abuse. Any caller can spend the operator's money, trigger rate-limit bans, or exfiltrate responses.
Security.fixed_code:
from fastapi import Depends, Header
import hmac

PROXY_API_KEY = os.environ.get("PROXY_API_KEY")

async def require_proxy_key(
    x_api_key: Optional[str] = Header(None, alias="x-api-key"),
    authorization: Optional[str] = Header(None),
):
    if not PROXY_API_KEY:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=503, detail="proxy auth not configured")
    candidate = x_api_key or (authorization or "").removeprefix("Bearer ").strip()
    if not hmac.compare_digest(candidate, PROXY_API_KEY):
        raise HTTPException(status_code=401, detail="invalid api key")

@app.post("/v1/messages", dependencies=[Depends(require_proxy_key)])
async def create_message(request: MessagesRequest, raw_request: Request):
    ...
Security.explanation: The proxy holds spend-bearing credentials, so it must demand a credential of its own. hmac.compare_digest avoids timing attacks.

Security.title: Server-side request forgery (SSRF) via configurable base_url
Security.description: `providers.*.base_url` is read from `config.json` with no scheme/host validation. An attacker who can write to the config can point `base_url` at internal URLs (cloud metadata services, internal admin panels).
Security.impact: SSRF lets an attacker probe internal network endpoints using the proxy's identity, potentially reaching cloud metadata services that leak IAM credentials.
Security.explanation: Validates the base URL at startup against a deny-list of private/link-local IP ranges. Defense in depth for shared-host threat models.

Security.title: Client-supplied API key forwarded directly to upstream providers (auth bypass)
Security.description: When `preferred_provider == "anthropic"`, the server reads `x-api-key` or `authorization: Bearer ...` from the inbound request and uses it verbatim as the upstream Anthropic credential, bypassing operator-controlled credentials.
Security.impact: Authorization bypass: an attacker can substitute their own Anthropic key and have the proxy route traffic through it, hiding their origin.
Security.explanation: The inbound credential should never determine the upstream credential. If client-key forwarding is desired, make it an explicit documented feature with audit logging.

Security.title: Missing rate limiting and request-size limits on proxy endpoints
Security.description: No rate limiter, no request body size cap, no per-IP concurrency cap. A single client can flood `/v1/messages` with large payloads and trigger expensive upstream calls.
Security.impact: Cost amplification attacks: each request costs the operator money at the upstream. Without limits, a malicious or buggy client can drain quotas, hit rate limits, or trigger runaway bills.
Security.current_code:
@app.post("/v1/messages")
async def create_message(request: MessagesRequest, raw_request: Request):
Security.explanation: Per-IP rate limiting and an explicit body-size cap prevent both accidental and malicious overload.

Security.title: Traceback and internal model paths printed to stdout even in production
Security.description: Logging is set to WARNING but `traceback.format_exc()` and full exception dicts are emitted at ERROR, and the `print()` in `log_request_beautifully` (line 322) bypasses the logger entirely.
Security.impact: Operators frequently ship stdout/stderr to third-party services. Printing unmasked URLs or model IDs can leak into systems with weaker access controls.
Security.fixed_code:
def log_request_beautifully(method, path, claude_model, routed_model, num_messages, num_tools, status_code):
    status = ("OK" if status_code == 200 else str(status_code))
    logger.info("%s %s %s claude=%s routed=%s tools=%d msgs=%d",
                method, path, status, claude_model, routed_model, num_tools, num_messages)
Security.explanation: Replacing `print()` with `logger.info()` routes output through the standard logging pipeline where operators can configure handlers, redaction, and sampling.

Security.title: Config file loaded without validation; trusts arbitrary JSON structure
Security.description: `_load_config` opens and `json.load`s the file. Malformed config (wrong types, missing fields) is accepted silently. Downstream code uses `.get()` chains that silently swallow errors, returning `None` or empty strings, leading to requests leaking with empty credentials.
Security.impact: Silent misconfiguration is a security issue: it bypasses the assumption that `api_key` is present and authenticated.
Security.explanation: Pydantic validation surfaces every misconfiguration at startup with a precise error message.

Performance.title: Per-request OpenAI content simplification does double-iteration and is O(n^2) on message count
Performance.description: For every request, the code iterates over messages, then for each message iterates over content blocks, then over each tool-result sub-block. Multiple `isinstance(...)` re-checks re-walk the same data structure.
Performance.impact: Latency overhead per request, especially for tool-heavy Claude Code sessions.
Performance.explanation: Single-pass construction avoids the multiple reassignments and dead-branch checks.

Performance.title: No HTTP client reuse — every non-streaming request creates a new client
Performance.description: When using a custom `base_url`, `litellm.completion` creates an HTTP client per call. LiteLLM does some pooling but not always. For high-QPS deployments this manifests as TCP/TLS handshake overhead.
Performance.impact: Higher latency and resource use under load. Connection pooling reduces per-request time by 50-200ms for TLS-heavy upstreams.
Performance.fixed_code:
import httpx
http_client = httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=60.0, limits=httpx.Limits(max_keepalive_connections=20))
litellm_req["client"] = http_client
litellm.drop_params = True
litellm.request_timeout = 60
Performance.explanation: Passing a shared `httpx.AsyncClient` to LiteLLM reuses TCP/TLS connections.

Performance.title: Frozenset construction of large model lists happens at import time
Performance.description: `OPENAI_MODELS = frozenset(CONFIG.get("openai_models", [])) or {...hardcoded...}` constructs a frozenset from config and falls back to a hardcoded set. The fallback is re-frozen on every import.
Performance.fixed_code:
DEFAULT_OPENAI_MODELS = frozenset({"o3-mini", "o1", ...})
OPENAI_MODELS = frozenset(CONFIG.get("openai_models")) or DEFAULT_OPENAI_MODELS
Performance.explanation: Hoist the default to module level so the fallback frozenset is built once.

Performance.title: JSON serialization in streaming loop builds event strings with f-strings — no batching
Performance.description: Each SSE event is individually constructed as an f-string and yielded. For high-throughput streams, this means many small yields to the ASGI layer.
Performance.impact: Marginal throughput improvement for very long streams. Not a priority.
Performance.explanation: Buffering reduces ASGI send calls. Use bytes yields and tight JSON separators.

Code Quality.title: Single 961-line file mixes routing, conversion, streaming, logging, and config
Code Quality.description: Config loading, model-name mapping, Pydantic models, request/response conversion, SSE streaming, HTTP routes, error formatting — all in one file.
Code Quality.impact: Hard to navigate, hard to test in isolation, large diffs on every change.
Code Quality.fixed_code:
server.py          # only FastAPI app + routes (~100 lines)
config.py          # CONFIG loading + Pydantic config models
models.py          # Anthropic-format Pydantic models
converters.py      # convert_anthropic_to_litellm, convert_litellm_to_anthropic
streaming.py       # handle_streaming + SSE event helpers
routing.py         # _map_model_name, _is_*_model helpers
logging_setup.py   # MessageFilter, Colors, log_request_beautifully
Code Quality.explanation: Module boundaries align with the existing comment dividers in the file.

Code Quality.title: `handle_streaming` function is 120+ lines with deeply nested conditionals
Code Quality.description: The function has 5+ levels of nesting, mutates 6+ state variables, and has three near-duplicate `content_block_stop` emission patterns. Cyclomatic complexity is high.
Code Quality.impact: Bugs in streaming code are extremely hard to diagnose because they only manifest for specific interleavings of text and tool-call deltas.
Code Quality.fixed_code:
@dataclass
class StreamState:
    text_block_index: int = 0
    open_text_block: bool = False
    tool_blocks: Dict[int, int] = field(default_factory=dict)
    next_tool_index: int = 1
    output_tokens: int = 0
    stop_emitted: bool = False

async def _emit_event(event: str, data: dict) -> str: ...
async def _open_text_block(state): ...
async def _close_text_block(state): ...
async def _open_tool_block(state, ...): ...
async def _close_tool_block(state, ...): ...

async def handle_streaming(response_generator, request):
    state = StreamState()
    yield await _emit_message_start(request)
    ...
    async for chunk in response_generator:
        await _apply_chunk(chunk, state)
        async for ev in state.pending_events():
            yield ev
Code Quality.explanation: Decomposing the function into a state object + small emitters makes each piece testable and the streaming protocol invariants explicit.

Code Quality.title: Pydantic v1-style validators used with Pydantic v2
Code Quality.description: Mixing v1 `validator` patterns with v2 semantics. The `info.data` mutation pattern is broken in v2.
Code Quality.impact: Migration debt. Future maintainers will write more broken v1-style code following the existing pattern.
Code Quality.explanation: Use the v2-native `model_validator(mode="before")` for input transformation.

Code Quality.title: Duplicated dict-vs-attribute access patterns throughout converters
Code Quality.description: Code repeatedly does `if isinstance(x, dict): x.get("foo") else getattr(x, "foo", default)`. Repeated 10+ times.
Code Quality.impact: Maintenance burden. Easy to introduce subtle bugs (e.g., one path uses `getattr` with default `0`, another with `None`).
Code Quality.current_code: func = tool_call.get("function", {}) if isinstance(tool_call, dict) else getattr(tool_call, "function", None)
Code Quality.explanation: Two tiny helpers collapse the duplication and centralize the JSON-parse-on-string handling.

Code Quality.title: Bare `except:` clauses in parse_tool_result_content and convert_litellm_to_anthropic
Code Quality.description: Three uses of bare `except:` (catches BaseException including KeyboardInterrupt and SystemExit). Should be `except Exception`.
Code Quality.impact: Hides real bugs and makes Ctrl-C unreliable. PEP 8 explicitly recommends `except Exception`.
Code Quality.explanation: Replace bare except with `except Exception` to follow PEP 8.

Code Quality.title: `import traceback` duplicated in the exception handler
Code Quality.description: `traceback` is imported at module top and re-imported inside the function.
Code Quality.current_code:
import traceback
...
except Exception as e:
    import traceback
Code Quality.fixed_code:
except Exception as e:
    error_details = {"error": str(e), ...}

Best Practices.title: No structured logging, request IDs not propagated, no correlation across logs
Best Practices.description: Logs are plain string messages without structured fields, request IDs, or upstream latency breakdowns.
Best Practices.impact: Hard to operate. A single failed request generates 3-5 log lines but they can't be tied together.
Best Practices.fixed_code:
import structlog
log = structlog.get_logger()

request_id = raw_request.headers.get("x-request-id") or f"req_{uuid.uuid4().hex[:12]}"
log = log.bind(request_id=request_id, model=request.model, stream=request.stream)
log.info("processing")
Best Practices.explanation: Structured logging with a bound request_id lets operators grep one ID across client logs, proxy logs, and upstream traces.

Best Practices.title: No OpenTelemetry / Prometheus metrics for upstream latency, error rate, token usage
Best Practices.description: The proxy has no metrics. Operators can't see request rate, p50/p99 latency per provider, error rate, or token consumption without parsing logs.
Best Practices.impact: Can't alert on cost spikes, can't see which provider is slow, can't capacity-plan.
Best Practices.fixed_code:
from prometheus_client import Counter, Histogram, generate_latest

REQ_COUNT = Counter("proxy_requests_total", "Requests", ["provider", "model", "status"])
LATENCY = Histogram("proxy_request_duration_seconds", "Request duration", ["provider", "model"])
TOKENS = Counter("proxy_tokens_total", "Tokens consumed", ["provider", "model", "direction"])

@app.middleware("http")
async def metrics_middleware(request, call_next):
    start = time.time()
    response = await call_next(request)
    LATENCY.labels(provider=PREFERRED_PROVIDER, model="unknown").observe(time.time() - start)
    REQ_COUNT.labels(provider=PREFERRED_PROVIDER, model="unknown", status=response.status_code).inc()
    return response

@app.get("/metrics")
async def metrics():
    return Response(generate_latest(), media_type="text/plain")
Best Practices.explanation: A minimal Prometheus exposition gives operators the visibility they need.

Best Practices.title: Hardcoded host `0.0.0.0` and port `8082` ignore environment configuration
Best Practices.description: `uvicorn.run(app, host="0.0.0.0", port=8082, log_level="error")` ignores `HOST` and `PORT` environment variables.
Best Practices.impact: Container deployments often need to bind to a specific port from env. The current code requires patching the file.
Best Practices.fixed_code:
HOST = os.environ.get("HOST", "0.0.0.0")
PORT = int(os.environ.get("PORT", "8082"))
LOG_LEVEL = os.environ.get("LOG_LEVEL", "error")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    uvicorn.run(app, host=HOST, port=PORT, log_level=LOG_LEVEL)
Best Practices.explanation: Reads env vars consistent with `CONFIG_PATH`. Standard 12-factor pattern.

Best Practices.title: Missing type hints on internal helpers
Best Practices.description: Functions like `parse_tool_result_content`, `clean_gemini_schema`, `convert_litellm_to_anthropic`, `handle_streaming` use `Any` heavily. Better typing (TypedDict, dataclasses) would document the expected shape.
Best Practices.impact: Reduced IDE autocomplete, more bugs from wrong types slipping through.
Best Practices.explanation: TypedDict gives structural typing for internal dicts, helping mypy catch shape errors.

Best Practices.title: No CORS configuration — explicit opt-out would be clearer
Best Practices.description: FastAPI has no CORS middleware configured. By default it returns no `Access-Control-Allow-Origin`, so browser clients can't reach it.
Best Practices.impact: Surprise for browser-based clients. Operators may add ad-hoc CORS middleware, breaking in subtle ways.
Best Practices.fixed_code:
from fastapi.middleware.cors import CORSMiddleware

ALLOWED_ORIGINS = os.environ.get("ALLOWED_ORIGINS", "").split(",")
if ALLOWED_ORIGINS:
    app.add_middleware(
        CORSMiddleware,
        allow_origins=ALLOWED_ORIGINS,
        allow_methods=["POST", "GET"],
        allow_headers=["x-api-key", "authorization", "content-type", "anthropic-version"],
    )
Best Practices.explanation: Explicit env-driven CORS configuration. Default-off to preserve security; operators opt in deliberately.

Bugs.title: `info.data` used in Pydantic validator — unsupported API that silently drops `original_model`
Bugs.description: Pydantic v2 does not provide a writable `info.data` during field validation in the way the code assumes. Mutating `info.data["original_model"]` from a `@field_validator("model")` typically raises `ValueError` or silently does nothing.
Bugs.impact: The `original_model` field is populated based on the validator's mutation. If the mutation is a no-op, `original_model` is always `None`, and `count_tokens` and `create_message` will misreport and misroute the model name.
Bugs.explanation: Pydantic v2 forbids mutating other fields from a per-field validator. The supported pattern is `model_validator(mode="before")`.

Bugs.title: Streaming generator doesn't handle client disconnect or upstream cancellation
Bugs.description: `handle_streaming` is an async generator. If the client disconnects mid-stream, the underlying `response_generator` from `litellm.acompletion` is never cancelled — the upstream HTTP request keeps running until the upstream closes or times out.
Bugs.impact: Resource leak on every client disconnect — wastes upstream tokens (paid) and holds sockets open. On flaky networks, the client sees a malformed stream because open content blocks are never closed.
Bugs.fixed_code:
async def handle_streaming(response_generator, original_request: MessagesRequest):
    message_id = f"msg_{uuid.uuid4().hex[:24]}"
    text_block_closed = False
    ...
    try:
        # preamble events
        ...
        async for chunk in response_generator:
            ...
    except asyncio.CancelledError:
        logger.info("client disconnected, cancelling upstream stream")
        gen = response_generator
        if hasattr(gen, "aclose"):
            await gen.aclose()
        raise
    except Exception as e:
        logger.error("streaming error: %s", e, exc_info=True)
        # Close any open blocks before signalling error
        if not text_block_closed:
            yield "event: content_block_stop\n...\n\n"
        yield f"event: message_delta\n...\n\n"
        yield "event: message_stop\n...\n\n"
        yield "data: [DONE]\n\n"
    finally:
        gen = response_generator
        if hasattr(gen, "aclose"):
            try:
                await gen.aclose()
            except Exception:
                pass
Bugs.explanation: Honoring CancelledError and explicitly calling aclose() on the upstream generator ensures sockets and asyncio tasks are released.

Bugs.title: Streaming response can yield `message_stop` twice
Bugs.description: The terminal branch at line 712 emits `message_stop` and `[DONE]` then `return`s. The post-loop fallback at line 734 also emits `message_stop` and `[DONE]`. If an upstream `finish_reason` arrived but a chunk exception suppressed the terminal path, the post-loop block re-emits a second `message_stop`.
Bugs.impact: Subtle streaming protocol violation that breaks some SSE consumers. Hard to reproduce but visible in production logs.
Bugs.current_code:
if finish_reason and not has_sent_stop_reason:
    has_sent_stop_reason = True
    ...
    yield "data: [DONE]\n\n"
    return

if not has_sent_stop_reason:
    ...
    yield "data: [DONE]\n\n"
Bugs.fixed_code:
# Hoist the terminal emission into a helper, and only emit message_stop once.
async def _emit_terminal(stop_reason, output_tokens, tool_indices, text_closed):
    if not text_closed:
        yield "event: content_block_stop
...

"
    for i in tool_indices:
        yield f"event: content_block_stop
...

"
    yield f"event: message_delta
...

"
    yield "event: message_stop
...

"
    yield "data: [DONE]

"

# In main loop, set has_sent_stop_reason immediately on first terminal emission.
# In the post-loop fallback, check has_sent_stop_reason and skip if already set.
Bugs.explanation: Centralizing the terminal SSE events into a helper and gating the post-loop fallback eliminates the duplicate-stop and malformed-stream cases.

Bugs.title: Synchronous `litellm.completion()` called inside an async endpoint blocks the event loop
Bugs.description: `create_message` is declared `async def` but the non-streaming path calls `litellm.completion(**litellm_req)` synchronously. This blocks the asyncio event loop for the entire duration of the upstream call.
Bugs.impact: Production-grade async proxies must use `litellm.acompletion` everywhere. The current code works for one slow request at a time but degrades catastrophically under concurrent load.
Bugs.explanation: `litellm.acompletion` is the async equivalent and releases the event loop while waiting on the upstream.

Bugs.title: `isinstance(content, list)` check on line 853 always False because content was just set to str
Bugs.description: After `text_content.strip() or "..."` is assigned back to `msg["content"]`, the code checks `if isinstance(msg.get("content"), list)` — but the previous line just assigned a string. The condition is always False.
Bugs.impact: Dead code — confusing to maintainers and indicates the intent was different from what was implemented.
Bugs.fixed_code:
litellm_req["messages"][i]["content"] = text_content.strip() or "..."

# Optional safety net:
if msg.get("content") is None:
    litellm_req["messages"][i]["content"] = "..."
Bugs.explanation: Removing the dead branches clarifies the actual behavior.

Bugs.title: `convert_anthropic_to_litellm` may emit unsupported params for non-Anthropic models
Bugs.description: `system_content` is added unconditionally. Some non-Anthropic providers don't accept a top-level `system` key. `drop_params=True` is not set for the OpenAI route at line 794-801.
Bugs.impact: Intermittent upstream 400s for OpenAI/Google routes when the Anthropic request includes a system prompt.
Bugs.explanation: Setting `drop_params=True` unconditionally tells LiteLLM to drop unsupported params instead of erroring.

Bugs.title: Fallback `token_counter` import error returns a fixed value of 1000
Bugs.description: If `token_counter` is not importable, the function returns `TokenCountResponse(input_tokens=1000)` silently. Claude Code may make token-counting decisions based on this arbitrary number.
Bugs.impact: Silent incorrect behavior. Better to raise or return None and let the caller decide.
Bugs.fixed_code:
except ImportError:
    raise HTTPException(status_code=503, detail="token counting unavailable")
Bugs.explanation: Fail loud rather than silently returning a wrong number.

Bugs.title: Content simplification for OpenAI strips images entirely (vision regression)
Bugs.description: When the routing target is OpenAI/Gemini and the inbound request includes an image content block, the simplification loop replaces the block with the literal string `"[Image content]"`. OpenAI can natively accept image content.
Bugs.impact: Any Claude Code workflow that includes images silently loses vision capability when routed through OpenAI.
Bugs.fixed_code:
# Pass through base64 images as image_url content parts:
if isinstance(block.get("source"), dict) and block["source"].get("type") == "base64":
    media_type = block["source"].get("media_type", "image/png")
    data = block["source"].get("data", "")
    msg["content"] = msg.get("content")  # keep as list
    # emit structured image_url content
text_content += "[Image content]\n"  # only as last resort
Bugs.explanation: Multimodal content should be passed through to providers that support it.

strengths: Model-name mapping is flexible and handles Anthropic/OpenAI/Gemini/Azure/Vertex/custom providers with a clean prefix-based abstraction
Streaming output produces well-formed Anthropic SSE events with correct content_block indices and tool_use handling
Pydantic models mirror the Anthropic API surface accurately (ContentBlockText, ContentBlockImage, ContentBlockToolUse, ContentBlockToolResult)
Logging filter (MessageFilter) actively suppresses noisy LiteLLM internal logs without losing genuine warnings/errors
Color-coded request logging is helpful for local development and easy to disable
Tool result content normalization handles string/list/dict inputs uniformly via parse_tool_result_content
Custom base_url support enables BYO OpenAI-compatible endpoint without forking
Streaming entry point correctly emits message_start, content_block_start, and ping events per Anthropic spec

quick_wins.title: Replace sync litellm.completion with litellm.acompletion in non-streaming path
Add PROXY_API_KEY auth via FastAPI Depends
Remove client-key fallback for Anthropic upstream
Sanitize exception objects before logging (deny-list api_key/authorization)
Add CancelledError handler + aclose() in handle_streaming
Add SSRF deny-list validation for base_url
Remove dead isinstance(list) branch in OpenAI content simplification
Replace print() in log_request_beautifully with logger.info
Replace bare except: with except Exception:

refactoring_opportunities: Split server.py (961 lines) into config.py, models.py, converters.py, streaming.py, routing.py, logging_setup.py
Decompose handle_streaming (120+ lines, 5 nesting levels) into a StreamState dataclass + small emitters (_open_text_block, _close_text_block, _open_tool_block, _close_tool_block)
Introduce _maybe() and _func_args() helpers to eliminate the 10+ duplicated isinstance-dict/getattr access patterns
Migrate remaining v1-style @field_validator patterns to v2-native @model_validator(mode='before') for input transformation
Wrap the OpenAI content simplification in a single-pass helper _simplify_for_openai() and remove dead branches
Hoist DEFAULT_OPENAI_MODELS frozenset to module level
Use TypedDict for internal helper signatures (parse_tool_result_content, clean_gemini_schema)
Add a shared httpx.AsyncClient for connection reuse
